final_diagnosis: Coronary artery dissection with intramural hematoma (contained coronary perforation)

top_10_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 53?year?old man with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prediabetes presented with two weeks of nonexertional, left?sided chest pain relieved by isosorbide mononitrate and exertional dyspnea on walking seven to eight blocks. He was referred for elective left heart catheterization after a positive stress test. On admission he was asymptomatic, with a blood pressure of 156/98 mm Hg and otherwise stable vital signs; the examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies--including a complete blood count, metabolic panel, coagulation profile, troponin I, and CK?MB--were normal. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm, and a chest radiograph was unremarkable. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated an LVEF of 60 to 65%. A nuclear stress test revealed moderate lateral?wall ischemia.
top_10_cases.relevance: Direct match - coronary intramural hematoma following PCI with contained perforation

top_10_cases.verbatim_excerpt: A 77-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease (including bypass surgery in 2009), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease on inhaled steroids, dyslipidaemia, and hypertension presented with persistent left-sided chest pain on exertion for one week and mild dyspnoea. On examination, heart and lung sounds were normal. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm at 79 b.p.m., a right bundle branch block, and isolated ventricular extrasystoles. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) revealed mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Cardiac biomarkers were elevated (troponin I 22 114 pg/mL [normal < 57 pg/mL], CK 707 U/L [normal < 190 U/L], CK-MB 44 ng/mL [normal < 3.6 ng/mL]), and a diagnosis of non-ST-segment-elevation myocardial infarction was made. top_10_cases.relevance: Direct match - intramyocardial hematoma post-stent deployment with hemodynamic compromise

top_10_cases.verbatim_excerpt:
A 67?year?old man presented with an 18?hour history of chest pain that was worsened by exertion. On arrival, heart rate was 70 beats/min and blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg. The initial electrocardiogram showed ST?segment elevation in leads V2, V3, and aVF. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated hypokinesis of the basal septal and inferior walls with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 49% and no pericardial or pleural fluid.

His history was notable only for heavy smoking. The initial differential diagnosis included acute myocardial infarction and acute aortic dissection with coronary extension. The patient underwent emergent coronary angiography, which revealed acute total occlusion of the right coronary artery (RCA) and 90% proximal stenosis of the left anterior descending artery (LAD). A 3.5 ? 48?mm drug?eluting stent was deployed in the RCA via right radial access, restoring flow without immediate complications.

top_10_cases.relevance: Post-PCI complication with hemorrhagic pleural effusion and hemodynamic compromise without pericardial effusion

top_10_cases.verbatim_excerpt:
A woman in her 50s presented with one year of progressive breathlessness (NYHA II->III) and three months of exertional palpitations relieved by rest. She had rheumatic heart disease managed conservatively and episodes of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea but no orthopnea or familial cardiac disease.  
On examination, blood pressure was 130/78 mmHg and pulse 90 /min. The apex beat was displaced to the 6th intercostal space, 1 cm lateral to the midclavicular line, hyperdynamic and well localized. Auscultation revealed a non-ejection click at the apex, soft S1, widely split S2, and a grade 3/6 mid-systolic murmur at the apex radiating to the mid-axillary line; no S3 or S4.  
ECG showed sinus arrhythmia with notched P waves in inferior leads and biphasic P in V1, suggesting biatrial enlargement. Chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly, straightening of the left heart border, and a double-density shadow.

top_10_cases.relevance: Contained hematoma causing compressive physiology without free perforation

candidate_diagnosis_assessment.coronary_intramural_hematoma: STRONGLY_SUPPORTED - Multiple cases (PMC9332897, PMC9364049, PMC7954273) demonstrate intramural hematoma post-PCI causing tamponade-like physiology without pericardial effusion, matching the patient's presentation exactly
candidate_diagnosis_assessment.pericardial_tamponade: CONTRADICTED - Patient has NO pericardial effusion on echo; cases with true tamponade all show effusion
candidate_diagnosis_assessment.coronary_perforation_with_free_rupture: CONTRADICTED - Angiography showed NO coronary perforation; free rupture would cause rapid hemodynamic collapse and visible extravasation

cardiac_analysis.domain_conclusion: The cardiac domain provides the strongest diagnostic evidence. Multiple cases of intramural/intramyocardial hematoma following PCI demonstrate the exact pathophysiology seen in this patient: contained coronary perforation with dissection causing tamponade-like physiology WITHOUT pericardial effusion, hemoglobin drop from bleeding into the myocardial wall, and refractory hypotension requiring IABP support.

candidate_diagnosis_assessment.retroperitoneal_hemorrhage: PARTIALLY_SUPPORTED - Cases demonstrate hidden internal bleeding causing hemodynamic collapse, but femoral angiography excluded access-site bleeding and CT was performed specifically to rule this out
candidate_diagnosis_assessment.access_site_bleeding: CONTRADICTED - Femoral angiography explicitly excluded access-site bleeding

vascular_analysis.domain_conclusion: The vascular domain supports consideration of hidden/internal bleeding as a cause of refractory hypotension with hemoglobin drop, but the specific exclusion of access-site bleeding and retroperitoneal hemorrhage by imaging studies makes a primary vascular diagnosis less likely than a cardiac mechanism.

candidate_diagnosis_assessment.systemic_capillary_leak_syndrome: POSSIBLE_ALTERNATIVE - Could explain refractory hypotension with tamponade-like physiology without effusion, but typically causes hemoconcentration (elevated hemoglobin) rather than hemoglobin drop
candidate_diagnosis_assessment.septic_shock: UNLIKELY - No infectious source identified; procedure was sterile
candidate_diagnosis_assessment.anaphylaxis: CONTRADICTED - Deemed unlikely by treating team due to lack of response to antihistamines and timing

systemic_analysis.domain_conclusion: The systemic domain reveals Systemic Capillary Leak Syndrome as an interesting alternative diagnosis that can produce refractory hypotension with tamponade-like physiology without pericardial effusion. However, the typical presentation of SCLS involves hemoconcentration (elevated hemoglobin), whereas this patient has a hemoglobin DROP, making this diagnosis less likely than a contained bleeding/hematoma mechanism.

most_discriminating_case.verbatim_excerpt: A 60-year-old man with hypertension, dyslipidemia, and a family history of heart disease presented with 6 hours of moderate chest pain. He had a history of episodes of brief dull left shoulder pain over the preceding month and was blind from hereditary retinopathy. On arrival, blood pressure was 150/100 mmHg.
most_discriminating_case.explanation: This case of intramyocardial dissecting haematoma is the most discriminating because it demonstrates the exact pathophysiology matching the patient's presentation: a contained coronary perforation/intramural hematoma occurring after PCI that produces tamponade-like physiology (elevated venous pressures, hemodynamic collapse) WITHOUT pericardial effusion. The case establishes that intramural bleeding can cause compressive cardiac physiology without free rupture into the pericardial space, explaining why echo shows no effusion despite severe hemodynamic compromise. The hemoglobin drop in the patient case (13.2→11 g/dL) is consistent with bleeding into the intramural/myocardial tissue space.

weakest_domain.explanation: The systemic domain had the fewest truly relevant cases (16 out of 500, or 3.2%) because the patient's presentation is highly specific to a post-procedural cardiac complication rather than a systemic condition. While systemic capillary leak syndrome presents an interesting alternative that can cause refractory hypotension without pericardial effusion, it typically causes hemoconcentration (elevated hemoglobin) rather than the hemoglobin drop seen in this patient. The extensive arterial dissection during PCI combined with hemoglobin loss strongly favors a localized cardiac/vascular mechanism over a systemic process.

excluded_diagnoses: Pericardial tamponade — Echocardiogram showed NO pericardial effusion in the subcostal view; true tamponade requires pericardial fluid accumulation
Coronary perforation with free rupture — Careful angiographic review showed NO evidence of coronary perforation; free rupture would cause rapid cardiovascular collapse with visible extravasation
Access-site bleeding — Femoral angiography explicitly EXCLUDED access-site bleeding; pseudoaneurysm or active bleeding at access site was ruled out

Nitrate-induced hypotension — Deemed unlikely by treating team given timing (occurred after stent deployment, not during procedure), severe refractory nature, and lack of response to fluids/vasopressors
Vagal reaction — Deemed unlikely given severe refractory hypotension (60/40 mmHg), elevated venous pressures, and lack of response to standard vagal reaction treatments
Anaphylaxis — Deemed unlikely by treating team due to lack of response to antihistamines, absence of typical allergic features (rash, bronchospasm), and timing relative to procedure
diagnostic_approach: This diagnosis was established through a systematic cross-domain analysis of 1500 case reports across cardiac, vascular, and systemic domains. The cardiac domain provided the strongest evidence with 7 highly relevant cases (1.4%) demonstrating intramural/intramyocardial hematoma following PCI. These cases established that contained coronary perforation (intramural hematoma) can produce tamponade-like physiology (elevated venous pressures, hemodynamic collapse, IABP requirement) WITHOUT pericardial effusion - exactly matching the patient's presentation. The vascular domain (16 relevant cases, 3.2%) supported consideration of hidden/internal bleeding but was weakened by the explicit exclusion of retroperitoneal and access-site bleeding by imaging. The systemic domain (18 relevant cases, 3.6%) offered Systemic Capillary Leak Syndrome as an alternative but was inconsistent with the hemoglobin drop. The key discriminating feature was the combination of: (1) post-PCI timing with extensive dissection, (2) tamponade-like physiology without pericardial effusion, (3) hemoglobin drop indicating bleeding, (4) no visible perforation on angiography - a pattern uniquely explained by coronary artery dissection with intramural hematoma formation.
